title: Cat Screaming When Picked Up Under Belly: Emergency Guide
meta_desc: If your cat screams when lifted by the abdomen, they are in acute pain. Learn about potential causes and why this is a high-priority emergency.
content_html:
TL;DR: If your cat screams when touched or lifted under the belly, it is a high-priority veterinary emergency requiring immediate medical attention. This behavior often indicates severe internal pain or life-threatening conditions like a urinary blockage.

Why does my cat scream when I touch or lift them by the belly?

  • Vocalizing or screaming when touched is a significant clinical sign that a cat's pain has become overwhelming.
  • This reaction typically suggests issues with internal organs like the bladder, kidneys, or intestines.
  • It can also indicate severe problems with the spine and surrounding musculature.

How urgent is it if my cat vocalizes from abdominal pain?

  • This situation is a high-priority veterinary emergency; contact your nearest emergency veterinarian immediately.
  • Vocalizing from abdominal pressure can signal life-threatening conditions like urinary blockages, acute pancreatitis, peritonitis, or internal trauma.
  • Many of these conditions cause a rapid decline if not treated within hours.

How can a photo or video help the veterinary triage team?

  • If safe, take a video of your cat's posture or reaction to provide vital triage clues.
  • The team can observe if the cat is "guarding" their belly, walking with a hunched back (kyphosis), or straining to urinate.
  • Visual evidence helps the staff determine urgency and prepare diagnostic tools like ultrasound or X-rays before you arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

When a cat screams upon being picked up under the belly, the owner should first ensure their own safety by adequately restraining the cat, as the behavior may stem from fear or pain. A light cloth placed over the cat's head can lessen external stimuli, and the cat can be transported in a dark box with adequate ventilation to minimize stress. During transport, minimize motion of the head, neck, and spine using a firm support. At the initial veterinary call, be prepared to describe the cat's level of consciousness, breathing pattern, and any known injuries.

Chapter: Emergency, General Principles, Cardiopulmonary

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1659)

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of abdominal pain in cats?
faqs.answer: Common causes include urinary tract obstructions, severe constipation or megacolon, pancreatitis, ingestion of a foreign object, or internal infections like pyometra in unspayed females.

faqs.question: Should I try to palpate the belly to find the source of pain?
faqs.answer: No. Avoid pressing on the abdomen as this can cause further injury, potentially rupture a delicate organ like the bladder, or cause the cat to bite out of fear and pain.

faqs.question: How should I move my cat to the vet?
faqs.answer: Try to minimize contact with the belly. Use a 'towel wrap' or gently slide the cat into a carrier by tilting the carrier or using a flat board to avoid putting pressure on the sensitive abdominal area.
